summary: Large formatting-only cleanup centered on fighter MotionState tables, expanding dense one-line table entries into consistently multi-line initializer blocks. The PR body also says it ran `make format`, which explains additional mechanical formatting churn in GX jump tables, ground/stage files, baselib, item, collision, player, and other touched files. No review discussion was captured.
what_changed: Reformatted many fighter `*_Init.c` motion state tables from compact row initializers into one-field-per-line initializer blocks with trailing commas.
The shown `ftCl_Init_MotionStateTable` entries preserve the same motion IDs, flags, callbacks, NULL callback slots, and `ftCamera_UpdateCameraBox` pointers while only changing layout.
Ran `make format`, causing broad mechanical formatting changes beyond fighter tables, including GX jump table declarations in `GXAttr.c`, `GXPerf.c`, and `GXTexture.c`.
Updated `Cargo.lock` package versions for local tooling crates: `melee-replace-symbols` from `0.1.0` to `0.2.0` and `melee-utils` from `0.1.0` to `0.1.1`.
Touched 102 files with 10199 added and 2744 deleted lines, mostly because large table initializers expanded vertically rather than because of apparent logic changes.
smart_moves: Applied a uniform table formatting style across many fighters at once, reducing local inconsistency in a very repetitive data-heavy part of the codebase.
Preserved table order and raw constants in the visible diff, making the cleanup easier to verify as mechanical rather than semantic.
Used formatter-driven cleanup (`make format`) instead of hand-formatting only selected files, which helps normalize style across unrelated table and jump-table declarations.
The expanded initializer style makes each `MotionState` field visually distinct, which should reduce future mistakes when editing callback slots or motion-state flags.
decomp_lessons: For decomp data tables such as `MotionState` arrays, readability can matter as much as compactness: one-field-per-line entries make IDs, flags, command values, and callback functions easier to audit.
Large mechanical formatting PRs should be kept semantically boring; this slice provides evidence of layout changes but not intentional behavior changes.
When running project-wide formatters, expect incidental churn in unrelated systems such as GX jump tables, baselib headers, ground files, and item/player code.
Expanded tables are especially useful for future matching or cleanup work because repeated callback patterns and NULL slots become searchable and diff-friendly.
naming_conventions: Fighter init files follow `ft<character abbreviation>_Init.c`, such as `ftCl_Init.c`, `ftKb_Init.c`, `ftMh_Init.c`, `ftDk_Init.c`, and `ftGw_Init.c`.
Motion state tables are named with the character prefix plus `_Init_MotionStateTable`, e.g. `ftCl_Init_MotionStateTable`.
Motion callbacks retain the common suffix convention `_Anim`, `_IASA`, `_Phys`, and `_Coll`.
Common fighter callbacks use the `ftCo_` prefix, character-specific callbacks use prefixes such as `ftCl_` or `ftLk_`, and camera callbacks use `ftCamera_`.
Generated or address-like jump table labels in GX files retain the `jtbl_80xxxxxx` naming style and point at address-like helper labels such as `GXAttr_8033CA6C`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No direct matching tactic or assembly change was documented in comments; the visible changes are formatting-only.
Preserving exact initializer values and order is important for table data matching; even a formatting cleanup should avoid reordering entries unless separately justified.
For jump tables declared as `jtbl_t`, formatter output may split entries across lines or switch between brace styles; verify generated data/layout if the table affects matching.
Trailing commas in multi-line initializers make future edits smaller and reduce diff noise when appending or modifying table entries.
follow_up_queries: Find PRs that later edited `MotionState` table structure or field names after this formatting pass.
Search for later review comments about one-field-per-line `MotionState` initializer style.
Check whether the `melee-replace-symbols` 0.2.0 and `melee-utils` 0.1.1 updates corresponded to formatter behavior changes.
Compare future fighter init PRs against this PR to see if they reused the expanded `MotionState` table format.
